metadata.summary: Two-dimensional arrangement of user stories along journey (activities → tasks) and priority (essential → nice-to-have) axes, enabling slice-based release planning and stakeholder alignment.

content.title: User Story Mapping
content.summary: A two-dimensional arrangement of user stories along a horizontal user-journey axis (activities → tasks) and a vertical priority axis (essential → nice-to-have), producing a story map that reveals the complete user experience and enables slice-based release planning. The BA owns scope definition: turning the map into formal artifacts (BRD, BPMN, traceability matrix, JIRA epics).

why.p: Flat backlogs lose journey context, making release planning a guessing game. Story mapping surfaces the walking skeleton — the thinnest vertical slice that delivers a coherent end-to-end experience — and lets a mixed business/IT audience agree on release scope using a single shared artifact. BABOK v3 §10.46 names it as the canonical technique for linking stories to process steps and traceability.

list.item: Sprint-zero or pre-discovery session where the BA must convert a fuzzy idea into a stakeholder-signed release plan.
Brownfield project with a flat 200+ ticket backlog needing journey context before re-prioritization.
Compliance/regulated project where each story must trace to a process step, regulation clause, and acceptance criterion.
Vendor migration: the existing journey is the backbone, gap analysis fills the cells.
Stakeholder alignment with mixed business + IT audience who need to agree on a release slice.
Maintenance backlog of isolated bug fixes and tech debt — no journey, use WSJF or RICE instead.
Pure API/platform product with no end-user persona — model with C4 + use cases.
Team already on a stable release cadence with a healthy backlog and clear roadmap — rebuilding the map is sunk cost.
One-off internal automation (single user, single happy path) — a 5-step checklist beats a wall map.
Stakeholders cannot commit 2-4 contiguous hours — a half-attended map produces false consensus.

section.name: framework
section.p: The backbone is the sequence of user Activities (coarse-grained goals). Under each Activity sit Tasks (specific interactions). Under each Task stack Stories (user story cards) ordered by priority. Horizontal lines drawn across the full map define release boundaries.

rule.statement: Arrange user stories along two axes: horizontal = user journey (activities then tasks), vertical = priority (essential at top, nice-to-have at bottom).
rule.rationale: A flat backlog has no journey context, making release planning impossible. The two-axis structure reveals the walking skeleton — the thinnest complete slice — and lets any stakeholder see what each release delivers end-to-end.

rule.statement: Complete all five steps in order; skipping Step 4 (vertical priority ordering) before Step 5 (release slicing) produces release lines with no coherent narrative.
rule.rationale: BABOK v3 §10.46 requires the map to represent a complete user experience before slicing; partial maps yield partial release value and false sign-off.

list.item: Step 1 — Identify User Activities: map high-level goals in user-journey sequence (e.g., Browse → Select → Purchase → Track → Return).
Step 2 — Break Down into Tasks: for each Activity, list specific interactions the user performs (e.g., Purchase → Add to cart, Enter shipping, Enter payment, Confirm order).
Step 3 — Write User Stories: for each Task, write stories using "As a [persona], I want [goal] so that [benefit]" format.
Step 4 — Arrange Vertically by Priority: top = essential for minimum viable experience; bottom = nice-to-have.
Step 5 — Slice into Releases: draw horizontal lines to define Release 1 (walking skeleton), Release 2 (enhancements), Release 3 (polish).

rule.statement: Release 1 must deliver a complete user journey (thin vertical slice through all activities), not a complete implementation of one activity.
rule.rationale: A release that completes one activity fully but leaves other activities with no coverage cannot be user-tested end-to-end; the walking-skeleton check fails.

list.item: Horizontal slice — cut off entire activities when activities are independent (rarely the right choice for Release 1).
Vertical slice — thin cut through all activities delivering minimum viability (correct approach for MVP).
Mixed — combination; most common in practice.

rule.statement: Walk the Release-1 line vertically with a real user or surrogate before sign-off: read each cell as a contiguous narrative. If it does not form a coherent story, the slice is wrong.
rule.rationale: Jeff Patton's walking-skeleton check is the concrete test for release-1 coherence; narrative breakdown reveals missing tasks, not missing stories.

rule.statement: Convert each story map cell to formal BA artifacts: BRD section (problem, scope, AC), BPMN participant + task, RTM row (story_id, requirement_id, process_step, test_case_id).
rule.rationale: BABOK treats the map as input to Requirements Analysis and Design Definition (KA 5); without artifact translation the map is a workshop souvenir, not a BA deliverable.

rule.statement: Tag every story cell with persona_id and process_step_id at write time; retrofitting traceability after the workshop is the single biggest time sink in BA delivery.
rule.rationale: Without source tagging, the RTM must be reconstructed from memory — a lossy and slow process that delays sign-off.

antipattern.description: Capturing non-functional requirements (performance, security, accessibility, audit) as story map cells.
antipattern.reason: NFRs have no natural cell on the map and are routinely dropped in the vertical-slice mentality. Capture them in a parallel NFR register linked to the map header; review per release slice, not per cell.

antipattern.description: Treating a single-axis prioritized backlog as a story map.
antipattern.reason: A list with no horizontal journey axis loses the context that explains why stories exist; BAs cannot trace them to process steps or write a coherent RTM.

rule.statement: Require all five participant roles in the workshop: Product Owner, Business Analyst, Developer, UX Designer, and at least one key stakeholder; refuse to proceed if the decision-maker is absent.
rule.rationale: A half-attended map produces false consensus — absent stakeholders discover the release plan only at review, triggering scope changes that invalidate the map's value.

list.item: Duration: 2-4 hours for a complete map; break into two sessions if scope exceeds 5 activities.
Materials: large wall or whiteboard, sticky notes in 3 colors (activities/tasks/stories), markers.
Agenda: personas + goals (15 min) → backbone activities (30 min) → tasks (30 min) → stories (60 min) → vertical prioritization (30 min) → release slices (30 min).

antipattern.description: Flat backlog — stories written without journey context.
antipattern.reason: No horizontal axis means no way to detect missing tasks or trace requirements to process steps.

antipattern.description: Technical stories on the map ("As a system, I want to batch-process orders").
antipattern.reason: INVEST requires user perspective; technical stories belong in a separate architectural backlog, not the story map.

antipattern.description: No release lines defined — map becomes a prioritized list.
antipattern.reason: Without horizontal slice boundaries, the team has no commitment to what ships together and cannot plan iterations.
Workshops optimize the happy path; add a red-sticky row for exception paths below each task band so they are visible, not forgotten.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A BA-led story-mapping engagement maps cleanly to a 4-stage subagent pipeline: (1) elicitation prep — research stakeholders, prior artifacts, regulations; (2) workshop facilitation — structured prompts to extract activities/tasks/stories live, BA validates; (3) artifact translation — convert the map into BRD sections, BPMN, traceability matrix, JIRA epics; (4) validation loop — run an LLM critic over each story for INVEST + AC completeness, then a human BA sign-off.

rule.statement: Run story mapping as a four-stage subagent pipeline: elicitation prep → workshop facilitation → artifact translation → validation loop.
rule.rationale: Each stage requires different model capabilities (research, structured elicitation, formatting, critique); a single prompt produces weaker output than staged handoffs.

rule.statement: Do not let the agent decide release boundaries; slicing is a business and risk decision. Lock the agent to "suggest releases" mode, never "commit releases".
rule.rationale: Release boundaries require authority the agent does not have; auto-committed slices bypass stakeholder sign-off and invalidate the validation loop.

rule.statement: After story generation, run an INVEST critic pass: reject stories that are too large (epic in disguise) or non-independent ("as a system").
rule.rationale: LLMs produce stories that pass a superficial format check but violate INVEST; an explicit critic pass before BA acceptance catches these failures cheaply.

example.title: Facilitation prompt pattern
example.code: You are a BABOK-aligned BA facilitator. Persona pack: {personas}. Current backbone: {activities}. We are decomposing activity "{a}" into tasks. Ask 3 elicitation questions targeting the persona's goal, constraints, and exception path. Output JSON: {questions: [], suggested_tasks: []}. No prose. No invented tasks not grounded in a question.
example.note: Constraints the agent to grounded output only.

example.title: Translation prompt
example.code: Input: story-map.json (activities, tasks, stories, releases). For each Release-1 story emit: BRD section (problem, scope, AC), BPMN participant + task, RTM row (story_id, requirement_id, process_step, test_case_id). Flag any story missing AC or persona reference. Output strict JSON matching schema {brd, bpmn, rtm, gaps}.
example.note: Enforces full BA artifact output with gap detection.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: LLMs invent activities to fill obvious-looking gaps in a journey. Constrain the facilitator agent: "only emit activities grounded in a stakeholder quote or prior artifact"; require source citation per node.

Story generation drifts from INVEST: agents produce stories that are too large (epics in disguise) or technical ("As a system, I want…"). Run an INVEST critic pass with a strict checklist before accepting any agent output.
Acceptance criteria from LLMs default to happy-path Given/When/Then. Force a structured prompt: "emit ≥1 happy, ≥1 alternate, ≥1 error AC; cite the persona's exception scenario from elicitation".
Map JSON schemas drift between tools (Miro, Jira, FeatMap). Pin one canonical schema in your repo; convert on the boundary. Otherwise downstream BRD/RTM renderers break silently.
Persona leakage: agents conflate personas across stories ("As a user…"). Enforce a persona_id enum in the schema and reject stories without it.
Token blow-up on large maps (200+ stories). Render BRD per activity, not per map; chunk the agent input to one activity at a time and concatenate.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: The map is a snapshot. Without a discipline of re-mapping every release, it diverges from reality within approximately 6 weeks.
Vertical "priority" axis collapses MoSCoW + value + risk + sequencing into one column — BAs frequently lose the why behind the order. Keep a separate prioritization matrix.

LLMs hallucinate plausible-but-fake user activities when the persona is thin. The walking-skeleton check (can a real user complete the flow end-to-end?) breaks if the map was written from imagination.
Cross-functional (B2B2C, marketplace, multi-actor) journeys do not fit a single backbone. You need swimlane variants per persona, which most tools do not render cleanly.
Non-functional requirements (performance, security, accessibility, audit) have no natural cell on the map and routinely get dropped — they must be captured in a parallel NFR register.

rule.statement: Enforce four mandatory human checkpoints: backbone approval before tasks, Release-1 slice approval before stories, BRD+RTM diff review before publication, NFR register review before sign-off.
rule.rationale: Each checkpoint prevents downstream artifact cascades from building on a flawed foundation; skipping any one checkpoint increases rework cost by at least one stage.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Run a pre-mortem in the first 15 minutes: ask "what would make this map useless in 3 months?". Capture answers as risks; assign owners. Maps die from neglect, not from bad structure.
Keep two priority axes, not one: vertical = release slice, color/badge = MoSCoW. Single-axis maps lose the regulatory "must" stories under "nice-to-haves" the loudest stakeholder pushed up.

The BA, not the PO, owns the persona pack going into the workshop. If personas are not validated against real research (≥5 interviews per persona), the map is fiction — flag this in the BRD assumptions section.
Tag every story cell with persona_id + process_step_id at write time. Retrofitting this for the RTM after the workshop is the single biggest time sink in BA delivery.

Capture the exception/unhappy paths on a separate row band below the main tasks — same activities, red stickies. Workshops chronically optimize the happy path and ship products that fail under real conditions.

Walk the Release-1 line vertically with a real user (or surrogate) before sign-off: read each cell aloud as a contiguous narrative. If it does not form a coherent story, the slice is wrong, not the stories.
Park NFRs (perf, sec, a11y, audit) in a sidebar register linked to the map header; review them per release slice, not per cell.
Version the map JSON in git alongside the BRD. The diff between v1 and v2 is the change request.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: Tools for rendering and managing story maps include featmap-cli (headless story-map render to SVG/JSON), storiesonboard-api (read/write StoriesOnBoard maps via REST), jira-cli (push map cells as Epics/Stories with parent links), bpmn-js-cli (convert task list to BPMN 2.0 XML headless), python-docx + Jinja2 (BRD/SRS rendering from map JSON), mermaid-cli (render journey diagrams from map backbone), and pandoc (map markdown to DOCX/PDF stakeholder package).

SaaS and OSS platforms include StoriesOnBoard (purpose-built for story mapping, REST API + webhook, Jira sync), FeatMap (lightweight OSS, JSON export, self-host), Avion (strong UX for live workshops, Jira sync), Miro (most common in workshops, REST API, sticky-note granularity), Mural (better outline to map conversion, REST API + OAuth), Jira + Easy Agile User Story Maps (map lives inside Jira, BA's RTM source of truth), Azure DevOps + Delivery Plans (common in regulated enterprises, REST API), Lucidchart / Lucidspark (useful when map to BPMN conversion is required, REST API), and Confluence (where the BRD + RTM end up, REST API).

template.purpose: Story map document template with backbone, walking skeleton, and per-activity release tables.
User story template with Given/When/Then acceptance criteria structure.
